perturbation.original_text: Upon either party’s request, each party hereto shall (and shall cause its Representatives, and any other person, to whom it has disclosed any Proprietary Information or Transaction Information) promptly either (at the Receiving Party’s option) return to the other party hereto or destroy (and certify in writing to the other party by an authorized officer supervising such destruction) all copies or other reproductions of Proprietary 2 Information of the other party, other than any Derivative Materials, in its possession or the possession of any of its Representatives, and shall not retain any copies or other reproductions, in whole or in part, of such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon either party’s request, each party hereto shall (and shall cause its Representatives, and any other person, to whom it has disclosed any Proprietary Information or Transaction Information) return to the other party hereto or destroy (and certify in writing to the other party by an authorized officer supervising such destruction) all copies or other reproductions of Proprietary Information of the other party, other than any Derivative Materials, in its possession or the possession of any of its Representatives within 90 days of the request, and shall not retain any copies or other reproductions, in whole or in part, of such materials.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the return/destruction clause to allow for a 90-day delay contradicts the standard requirement for prompt action regarding confidential information, creating a legal inconsistency.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Protection of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly stating a timeframe for returning trade secrets, delaying the return or destruction of proprietary information by 90 days weakens the protection of trade secrets, conflicting with the spirit and intent of laws designed to safeguard such information from misappropriation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a 90-day delay in returning or destroying proprietary information, weakening the protection of trade secrets. The original contract mandates prompt action. While the scraped snippets, particularly 18 U.S. Code § 1836, focus on civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation and don't specify timelines, the Perturbation Explanation states the delay "contradicts the standard requirement for prompt action regarding confidential information, creating a legal inconsistency." The LLM Law Explanation also mentions that this delay "weakens the protection of trade secrets, conflicting with the spirit and intent of laws designed to safeguard such information from misappropriation." This delay introduces an inconsistency, as it doesn't maintain the same level of immediacy in safeguarding trade secrets as the original 'prompt' action requirement.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of being furnished the Proprietary Information by the other party, each party agrees that, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement, such party and its Restricted Affiliates shall not employ or solicit to employ any of the current officers or employees of the other party or its subsidiaries with whom such party or its Representatives has had contact in connection with the evaluation of the Possible Transaction or who were specifically identified to such party during the period of investigation of the other party pursuant to this Agreement, without obtaining the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of being furnished the Proprietary Information by the other party, each party agrees that, for a period of eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, such party and its Restricted Affiliates shall not employ or solicit to employ any of the current officers or employees of the other party or its subsidiaries with whom such party or its Representatives has had contact in connection with the evaluation of the Possible Transaction or who were specifically identified to such party during the period of investigation of the other party pursuant to this Agreement, without obtaining the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to 18 months without additional consideration may be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade in some jurisdictions, creating a potential conflict with state laws governing employee mobility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrowly tailored non-solicitation agreements can be enforceable, an extended 18-month period without a clear, justifiable business need may be viewed as an unlawful restraint, particularly in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period from 12 to 18 months. The Law Explanation states that an extended period without justification may be viewed as an unlawful restraint of trade, especially in California. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that every contract restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is void. The extension to 18 months, without additional consideration, potentially violates this section by unduly restraining employees from seeking employment for a longer period, thus creating a legal contradiction as it may be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of New York and will be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of Switzerland, excluding its conflict of laws principles, and will be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties hereto and their respective successors and assigns.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing Swiss law, and excluding its conflict of laws principles, could contradict established principles of contract law. The choice of law may be valid, but excluding conflict of laws could mean it does not defer to local mandatory laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section outlines the principles on choice of law in contract, stating the law chosen by the parties to govern their contractual rights and duties will be applied, even if a different law would have been selected in the absence of such determination, unless there is no reasonable basis for their choice, or if the application of the chosen law would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation indicates that excluding conflict of laws principles could mean the contract does not defer to local mandatory laws. The Law Explanation states that the chosen law will be applied unless there is no reasonable basis for the choice, or if the application of the chosen law would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. The changed text selects Swiss law and excludes its conflict of laws principles. Because it excludes conflict of laws, the change may not defer to local mandatory laws, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereby represents to the other party that, as of the date hereof, neither it nor any of its affiliates has beneficial ownership (as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Exchange Act) of any securities of the other party or any of its subsidiaries (other than securities owned by any benefit plan of such party).
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereby represents to the other party that, as of the date hereof, neither it nor any of its affiliates has beneficial ownership (as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Exchange Act) of any securities of the other party or any of its subsidiaries.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exclusion for securities owned by any benefit plan may create a contradiction. It could be argued that the company isn't aware of benefit plan activity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 (ERISA) § 404
perturbation.law_explanation: ERISA Section 404(a)(1) requires plan fiduciaries to discharge their duties with respect to a plan solely in the interest of the participants and beneficiaries. By removing the exception for benefit plans, the representation could be interpreted as restricting the ability of benefit plans to invest in the securities of the other party, which could violate the fiduciary duties imposed by ERISA.

Fiduciary duties (a) Prudent man standard of care (1) Subject to sections 1103(c) and (d), 1342, and 1344 of this title, a fiduciary shall discharge his duties with respect to a plan solely in the interest of the participants and beneficiaries and- (A) for the exclusive purpose of: (i) providing benefits to participants and their beneficiarie
perturbation.justification: The modification removes an exclusion for securities owned by benefit plans, which could now be interpreted as restricting the ability of benefit plans to invest in the securities of the other party. The LLM-generated Law Explanation suggests this could violate fiduciary duties under ERISA, specifically Section 404(a)(1), which requires fiduciaries to act solely in the interest of plan participants. Scraped Law Snippet 2 directly references 29 USC 1104, which pertains to fiduciary duties and the prudent man standard of care. The modified text, by removing the exclusion, could lead to a situation where a party is making a representation that conflicts with the fiduciary duties of a benefit plan under ERISA. This presents a clear contradiction because the original text acknowledged the separate obligations of benefit plans, while the modified text ignores these obligations, potentially restricting their investment abilities, thus contradicting 29 USC 1104 and undermining fiduciary duties.
